title: Malevolent Hiding Hand

content.statement: Underestimation pairs with failure when creativity does not arrive in time.
content.question: On my biggest project, do I have honest outside-view estimates and pre-set kill-criteria?
content.equation: Failure Risk = Ambition × (1 ÷ Creative Capacity) × Sunk Cost
content.mental_model: Bridge half-built when funding ran out.
content.business_lens: On mega-projects, run rigorous reference-class forecasting; use the outside view; build kill-criteria that survive sunk-cost emotion.
content.life_lens: Distinguish brave ambition from naive optimism; the difference is often pre-planned exits.
sop: Use outside-view estimates for cost and time
Reward early termination of failing projects
Avoid concentrating runway on one mega-bet
content.explainer: Flyvbjerg’s research found mega-project cost overruns averaging 50%+; some run 200–300%. Many succumbed to the Malevolent Hiding Hand. The defense is structural: outside-view estimation, pre-commit kill criteria, and stage-gates that resist sunk-cost defense.
questions: On my biggest project, do I have honest outside-view estimates and pre-set kill-criteria?
Which project have you kept funding past the point where an outsider, using only the numbers, would have called it dead?
If you compared your original estimate for your biggest current bet to reality today, what's the actual overrun, and did you set a kill-criterion before you started, or are you deciding as you go?

definitions.def: Estimating a project's cost or time by looking at how similar past projects actually went, instead of reasoning from this project's own details.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Ambition 8, Creative Capacity 3 (so 1 divided by 3, approximately 0.33), Sunk Cost pressure 7. Score = 8 times 0.33 times 7, approximately 18.5. Higher means the project is racing toward overrun or collapse because ambition and sunk-cost pressure are outrunning your ability to creatively adapt; lower means your creative capacity is keeping pace, or ambition and sunk cost pressure are both still modest.
content.closing_statement: Ambition without a kill-switch becomes a slow collapse.

caption:
Principle Nº 323: Malevolent Hiding Hand

Underestimation pairs with failure when creativity does not arrive in time.

Ask yourself: On my biggest project, do I have honest outside-view estimates and pre-set kill-criteria?
